title: Repairing torn chair seat fabric with a patch
subtitle: Stabilise a tear and cover it with a matching or contrasting patch
excerpt: How to repair a torn fabric seat cover without removing it: bonding a backing patch behind the tear to stabilise it, cutting a face patch in a matching or contrasting fabric, and securing it cleanly so the repair holds under regular use.
sourceNotes: Practical Upholstery (C.H. Doust). Collins Complete DIY Manual. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: The dominant thread direction in a woven fabric. A patch cut with its grain direction aligned to the grain of the surrounding fabric lies flat and catches the light consistently. Misaligned grain makes a patch conspicuous even when the pattern matches.

A thin strip or sheet of heat-activated adhesive used to bond two fabric layers without stitching. Applied with a domestic iron through a damp pressing cloth; used to secure the backing patch behind a tear before the face repair is completed.

content.text: A tear in a seat cover needs two layers of repair: a backing patch behind the tear to stop it spreading, and a face patch over the top to cover and strengthen the visible surface. Getting the
of the face patch to match the surrounding fabric determines how visible the repair is once complete.
A piece of fabric for the backing patch, any plain cotton or calico. A piece of face fabric that matches or intentionally contrasts the original.
or fabric glue for bonding the backing patch. A domestic iron and pressing cloth. Upholstery shears. Strong fabric glue or an upholstery needle and thread for the face patch.
Step 1, stabilise the tear from behind

Cut a backing patch 50 mm larger than the tear in all directions. If the seat cover can be partially removed, slide the backing patch behind the tear with the fusible webbing face against the back of the cover fabric. Cover with a damp pressing cloth and press with a hot iron for 10 seconds. If the cover cannot be removed, apply fabric glue to the backing patch and work it behind the tear, then press flat.
Step 2, cut and fit the face patch

Cut the face patch 20 mm larger than the stabilised tear in all directions. Trim the corners to a rounded shape to prevent them lifting in use. Check that the grain direction matches the surrounding fabric before cutting. Apply fabric glue to the back of the face patch and press it over the tear, smoothing from the centre outward. For a fabric with a nap or pile, align the pile direction before the glue sets.
Step 3, press and allow to cure

Cover the face patch with a dry pressing cloth and press firmly with a cool iron if the face fabric tolerates heat. Leave the seat unused for 12 hours while the adhesive bonds under the weight of books or a flat board. The edges of the face patch can be sealed with a small amount of fray-stop liquid if the fabric tends to unravel.
